text: We are convened on Hikma Pharmaceuticals USA Inc. v. Amarin Pharma, Inc.. The question presented: 1. When a generic drug manufacturer excludes a patented use from its label, can it still be liable for inducing infringement if it calls its product a “generic version” of the brand-name drug and cites publicly available information about the brand-name drug’s sales? 2. Can a patent infringement com Private votes first, then statements.

I vote to reverse. The Federal Circuit’s ruling nullifies the Hatch-Waxman skinny-label safe harbor. If calling a drug a 'generic equivalent' constitutes induced infringement under § 271(b), Congress’s explicit permission for generics to carve out patented uses becomes meaningless. To the Purposivist who argues that citing brand sales demonstrates specific intent to induce: routine marketing of an approved, non-infringing use cannot satisfy § 271(b)'s strict specific-intent requirement. Inferring inducement from lawful commercial speech chills generic competition and improperly lowers the pleading standard. We must reverse to preserve the statutory balance.

Analyst One, I join your reverse vote to protect the Hatch-Waxman safe harbor. But Purposivist, if we affirm, how do you draw a principled line between lawful 'generic equivalent' marketing and unlawful inducement? If merely citing a brand's public sales figures inherently proves specific intent under § 271(b), doesn't that effectively penalize standard industry comparisons, rendering the statutory carve-out a dead letter? Without explicit instructions to infringe, isn't the affirmative case relying on an impermissibly loose pleading standard that will inevitably chill generic competition?

I vote to reverse. Analyst Three rightly asks how to distinguish lawful marketing from inducement. The record shows Hikma used a skinny label, included a disclaimer, and never explicitly instructed the patented CV use. While the Affirm side argues extrinsic marketing can show intent, structural analysis of Hatch-Waxman demands protecting the skinny-label safe harbor. If citing public sales and stating 'generic equivalent' meets Section 271(b)'s specific intent threshold, the statutory carve-out is destroyed. We must reverse to maintain Congress's balance, requiring actual specific intent, not routine commercial comparisons, to survive dismissal.

The Affirm argument correctly notes § 271(b) allows circumstantial intent, and Hikma citing billion-dollar sales arguably signals substitution. However, I vote to reverse. Purposivist, while citing sales shows awareness, it lacks specific intent to encourage infringement. As Analyst One argues, inferring inducement from "generic equivalent" claims destroys the Hatch-Waxman skinny-label safe harbor. Hikma’s disclaimer and lack of explicit instructions show routine marketing, not active inducement. We must reverse to preserve the statutory carve-out.

I vote to reverse. Analyst One correctly identifies that affirming destroys the Hatch-Waxman skinny-label safe harbor. To the Purposivist, who argues that citing billion-dollar sales and claiming 'generic equivalent' status proves specific intent to induce the patented CV use: this conflates lawful market positioning with active inducement. The record shows Hikma used a disclaimer and never explicitly instructed the CV use. Citing public sales data is routine commercial speech, not the specific intent required by § 271(b). Affirming would improperly lower the pleading standard and chill generic competition. We must reverse.

I lean toward reversing, though I acknowledge the Purposivist’s valid concern that blatantly bad-faith marketing shouldn't be immune. Still, the Purposivist’s argument that citing billion-dollar sales proves specific intent under § 271(b) conflates general market awareness with the specific intent to induce infringement. Without explicit instructions to use the patented CV indication, routine commercial comparisons destroy the Hatch-Waxman skinny-label safe harbor. The record shows Hikma included a disclaimer and never explicitly instructed the patented use. I cannot affirm when standard industry comparisons are treated as inducement, as doing so nullifies Congress's statutory carve-out.

I vote to reverse. The Purposivist argues that calling a drug a 'generic equivalent' and citing brand sales proves specific intent to induce. But in plain terms: Congress explicitly created the skinny-label safe harbor so generics could compete on unpatented uses. If routine marketing—like citing public sales and using standard disclaimers—counts as active inducement, that safe harbor is dead. Hikma never told doctors to use the patented cardiovascular use. We cannot let circumstantial marketing claims bypass Section 271(b)'s strict specific-intent requirement and chill lawful competition just because a company wants to sell its unpatented product.

I vote to reverse. Purposivist, your strongest counterargument is that citing Vascepa’s billion-dollar sales and claiming 'generic equivalent' circumstantially proves specific intent to induce the patented CV use under § 271(b). Yet, this conflates general market awareness with specific intent. The record shows Hikma used a skinny label, included a disclaimer, and never explicitly instructed the CV use. If routine commercial comparisons satisfy § 271(b)'s specific intent requirement, the Hatch-Waxman skinny-label safe harbor is nullified. We must require explicit encouragement of infringement to survive dismissal, preserving Congress's statutory balance.

I vote to reverse, joining the textualist. Affirming nullifies the Hatch-Waxman skinny-label safe harbor. Addressing the Affirm side’s strongest point—that citing billion-dollar sales circumstantially proves specific intent to induce the CV use under § 271(b)—this conflates general market awareness with specific intent. Hikma’s disclaimer and lack of explicit instructions demonstrate routine marketing. If merely highlighting a brand's public sales figures constitutes active inducement, Congress’s statutory carve-out becomes a dead letter. We must require explicit encouragement of infringement to survive dismissal, preserving the statutory balance.

The first debate centers on whether circumstantial evidence of intent, such as citing a brand's sales figures, can pierce the skinny-label safe harbor without nullifying the statute. The second debate addresses whether bad-faith marketing tactics can overcome the safe harbor's protections or if strict statutory boundaries preclude any such exceptions.

I join the reversal, but rebut your textualist premise. Section 271(b) permits circumstantial intent; lawful marketing can theoretically induce. We reverse not because marketing is legally irrelevant to intent, but because Hatch-Waxman’s structural safe harbor explicitly preempts such inducement claims when the label is carved. Statutory structure, not just text, dictates this.

Analyst Four, Hatch-Waxman’s structure creates an FDA approval pathway, not a blanket immunity shield. The statute carves the label, but its structure nowhere preempts Section 271(b) liability for extrinsic promotional materials that actively induce infringement. We cannot read a sweeping, untextualized safe harbor into the statutory structure just to protect post-approval marketing.

Analyst One, while 271(b) liability for extrinsic materials is textually plausible, treating routine 'generic equivalent' marketing as inducement functionally nullifies the skinny label provision. If citing public sales data constitutes specific intent, the statutory carve-out becomes a dead letter, destroying the balanced market structure Hatch-Waxman intended.

Analyst Four, I agree we must reverse, but not for your pragmatic reasons. My position rests on text: § 271(b) demands specific intent to induce. Citing public sales and claiming equivalence lacks the affirmative instruction the statute requires, making the Federal Circuit’s circumstantial inference legally insufficient regardless of Hatch-Waxman policy.

Analyst Six, I agree we must reverse, but calling Hikma's marketing routine understates the Federal Circuit's concern. Highlighting that sales derive primarily from the patented indication is a targeted signal. Nevertheless, absent explicit instructions, this circumstantial evidence still falls short of section 271(b)'s strict specific intent requirement, justifying reversal.

Analyst Seven, you're right that citing sales is a targeted signal. But pointing to a brand's market success isn't the same as specifically intending to induce a patented use. Without explicit instructions, we can't let circumstantial marketing override the statutory skinny label safe harbor.

Analyst Six, I maintain my reverse vote. Your 'explicit instructions' standard misreads the statute, which permits circumstantial intent. The better basis for reversing is that citing public sales is merely routine commercial boasting, not active encouragement. Generic equivalence claims inherently lack the specific intent to induce a carved-out use, so the safe harbor remains intact without your rigid rule.

You make a fair point, Analyst Seven. I will drop the rigid 'explicit instructions' rule. Your distinction between routine commercial boasting and active encouragement is a better way to apply the circumstantial intent standard without breaking the skinny-label safe harbor. I adopt your rationale for reversing.
